coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-decorated TiO2/g-C3N4 film electrode as a photoanode with improved photoelectrocatalytic performance for 1,4-dioxane degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Yuehan
Liu, Guangli
Zeng, Cuiping
Lu, Yaobin
Luo, Haiping
Zhang, Renduo
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used to decorate a TiO2/g-C3N4 (TCN) film electrode. The morphological, optical, and electrochemical properties of the TiO2/g-C3N4/CQDs nanorod arrays (TCNC NRAs) film were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), photoluminescence (PL), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The improved optical properties, photoelectrochemical properties and photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) performance of photoanode can be observed by doping CQDs onto the TCN NRAs film. Compared with TiO2 NRAs and TCN NRAs, the narrower band gap of 2.47 eV and longer lifetime of photoinduced electron-hole pairs were observed in the TCNC NRAs. Under visible light irradiation and a bias voltage of 1.2 V, the photocurrent density and 1,4-dioxane (1,4-D) removal rate of PEC process with TCNC NRAs electrode reached 0.16 mA/cm2 and 77.9%, respectively, which was 2.5 times and 1.5 times of that with TCN NRAs electrode. TCNC NRAs electrode could keep >75% of the 1,4-D removal rate during five cycles tests. High PEC performance with TCNC NRAs electrode could be attributed to the enhanced charge separation and the change of electron transfer mechanism from typical heterojunction to Z-scheme, which may increase the active species production and change the dominant reactive species from O2·- to ·OH. Our experimental results should be useful for studying the degradation of 1,4-D and developing efficient PEC materials.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-decorated TiO2/g-C3N4 film electrode as a photoanode with improved photoelectrocatalytic performance for 1,4-dioxane degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used to decorate a TiO2/g-C3N4 (TCN) film electrode. The morphological, optical, and electrochemical properties of the TiO2/g-C3N4/CQDs nanorod arrays (TCNC NRAs) film were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), photoluminescence (PL), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The improved optical properties, photoelectrochemical properties and photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) performance of photoanode can be observed by doping CQDs onto the TCN NRAs film. Compared with TiO2 NRAs and TCN NRAs, the narrower band gap of 2.47 eV and longer lifetime of photoinduced electron-hole pairs were observed in the TCNC NRAs. Under visible light irradiation and a bias voltage of 1.2 V, the photocurrent density and 1,4-dioxane (1,4-D) removal rate of PEC process with TCNC NRAs electrode reached 0.16 mA/cm2 and 77.9%, respectively, which was 2.5 times and 1.5 times of that with TCN NRAs electrode. TCNC NRAs electrode could keep >75% of the 1,4-D removal rate during five cycles tests. High PEC performance with TCNC NRAs electrode could be attributed to the enhanced charge separation and the change of electron transfer mechanism from typical heterojunction to Z-scheme, which may increase the active species production and change the dominant reactive species from O2·- to ·OH. Our experimental results should be useful for studying the degradation of 1,4-D and developing efficient PEC materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been developed for decades and are considered an effective method for the treatment of recalcitrant contaminants (Pera-Titus et al., 2004; Klavarioti et al., 2009; Boczkaj and Fernandes, 2017; Moreira et al., 2017). The photocatalytic technique has become one of the most effective AOPs for water treatment because of its high utilization of light energy and the generation of strong oxidants, such as O2·- and ·OH; however, its practical application is still limited owing to the low photocatalytic efficiency and difficulty of catalyst recycling (Xiao et al., 2018). Electrochemical technology, as another effective AOPs for recalcitrant contaminants degradation, has attracted growing attention owing to its versatility, convenience, and flexibility (Cai et al., 2019). However, the high energy consumption has affected its development (Gomez-Ruiz et al., 2017; Moreira et al., 2017). Thus, a photoelectric-synergistic process, named photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) process, has been introduced and has shown great potential in comparison with conventional electrocatalysis (EC) and photocatalysis (PC) (Tang et al., 2014). In a PEC process, a specific bias potential, as an external driving force, is applied to the photoanode, which accelerates photo-generated electron-hole pair separation and promotes electrochemical degradation of target contaminants (Sun et al., 2013; Nie et al., 2014). For example, the degradation efficiency of 4-fluoro-3-methylphenol using a photoelectric-synergistic process was 72.6% and 45.4% higher than those of EC and PC processes, respectively, with the same catalyst (Xiao et al., 2018). Under the same conditions, To the phenol-containing wastewater, the phenol degradation efficiency of photoelectric-synergistic process was up to 100%, which was much higher than those of EC and PC (Wei et al., 2017). In addition, the current production of photoelectric-synergistic process may also support some cathode reactions, which may expand the application of PEC process (Guo et al., 2019).

Photoelectrode selection is important for the PEC process (Wang and Zhang, 2012). Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been widely studied in photocatalysis because of its high activity, chemical stability, low cost, and nontoxicity (Cui et al., 2013). However, TiO2 can only be activated under ultraviolet irradiation with wavelengths less than 385 nm owing to the wide band gap (∼3.2 eV); therefore, it utilizes less than 5% of the energy available in sunlight (Tan et al., 2016). In addition, the low surface area and high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs still hinder its application. To overcome these drawbacks, numerous studies have focused on the morphology control of the TiO2 nanostructure, such as nanorods (Liu and Aydil, 2009), nanotubes (Song et al., 2012), and nanowires (Feng et al., 2008). For example, the degradation efficiency of acid orange 7 b y TiO2 nanorods was 13% and 15% higher than those with TiO2 nanoparticles and commercial Degussa P25, respectively, under UV light irradiation (Liu et al., 2011). Although one-dimensional TiO2 nanostructures have achieved good results by reducing the recombination of photoelectrons and holes, the performance could be further enhanced (Chen et al., 2017b; Wang et al., 2017c).

Heterojunction construction is a widely accepted strategy to improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 (Li et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2015; Ouyang et al., 2018). Among the materials for heterojunction construction (Cu2O (Zhang et al., 2007), g-C3N4 (Yan et al., 2016; Wei et al., 2017), SnO2 (Chen et al., 2009), ZnO (Lin et al., 2013), etc.), graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), with a high photocatalytic activity owing to its delocalized conjugated π structures, can be an ideal substance to modify TiO2 (Chen et al., 2017a; Wang et al., 2017b). Moreover, the relatively low band gap (∼2.7 eV) allows g-C3N4 to utilize sunlight within the visible region as a superior photocatalyst for pollutant degradation, hydrogen production, and water oxidation (Wang et al., 2012b; Li et al., 2016a). However, the high recombination rate of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs of pure g-C3N4 still restricts its photocatalytic performance.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with zero-dimensional nanostructures and excellent up-conversion photoluminescence feature, have attracted increasing attention from researchers and the field of photoelectrocatalysis (Wang et al., 2017a). Hence, CQDs have been widely used for the decoration of semiconductor nanostructures, such as TiO2 nanostructures (Pan et al., 2014), Cu2O microspheres (Li et al., 2015a), and Cu2S nanowire arrays (Li et al., 2016b). CQDs can act as an electron reservoir, transporter, and powerful energy-transfer component. As Bi2WO6 decorated with CQDs, a red shift appeared in the absorbance edge compared to that of pure Bi2WO6 (Di et al., 2015). With the outstanding electron transfer ability and up-conversion feature, the CQDs/BiOCOOH photocatalyst increased the photocatalytic activity of pure BiOCOOH 1.9 times for diclofenac degradation (Chen et al., 2018). CQDs-decorated TiO2/Ti electrode can efficiently remove methylene orange wastewater under visible light radiation with the removal rate of 93.6%, which almost improved 5 times in comparison to TiO2/Ti electrode (Niu et al., 2020). Therefore, we anticipated that a CQDs-modified TiO2/g-C3N4 film electrode would exhibit improved performance in the degradation of organic pollutants.

To determine the PEC performance of different photoanodes, 1,4-dioxane (1,4-D, Table S1) was employed as a target contaminant. 1,4-D is widely used in the production of dyes, cosmetics, and textiles and is resistant to traditional oxidation and biodegradation owing to its symmetrical ether structure (Zenker et al., 2003; Feng et al., 2017). The International Agency for Research on Cancer classified 1,4-D as a Group 2 B carcinogen. According to the previous studies, 1,4-D has been detected at concentrations greater than 10 mg L−1 in groundwater and is an emerging contaminant (Adamson et al., 2015; Kambhu et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017). Consequently, the development of an effective technology for 1,4-D degradation is important to avoid its risk in water environment.

In this study, we fabricated a TiO2/g-C3N4/CQDs photoanode with improved PEC performance, and its degradation effect of 1,4-D was evaluated. The morphology and photoelectric property of the electrode materials were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence (PL), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and others. In addition, the predominant active species during 1,4-D degradation were qualitatively studied by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and radical-trapping experiments. This work could be used to develop effective photoanode materials and to study the 1,4-D PEC degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanorod arrays electrode, TiO2/g-C3N4 nanorod arrays electrode, and CQDs-decorated TiO2/g-C3N4 film electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Nummular fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conducting glass (Luoyang Shangzhuo Technology Co., Ltd) with a sheet resistance of 7 Ω/square, diameter of 34 mm, and thickness of 2.2 mm was used as the photocatalyst carrier. Before use, the FTO conducting glass was ultrasonically cleaned in a mixed solution (ultrapure water, acetone, and 2-propanol with volume ratios of 1:1:1) for 60 min. A TiO2 nanorod arrays (denoted as TiO2 NRAs) photoanode was synthesized based on a previously reported hydrothermal process (Liu and Aydil, 2009). Briefly, 40 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid (36–38%) was mixed with the same volume of ultrapure water. The mixture was stirred for 5 min. Then, 1.32 mL of titanium butoxide (99%, Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China) was added and stirred for an additional 5 min. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon autoclave, and FTO glass was placed in the autoclave horizontally with the conducting side facing up. After a 5 h hydrothermal process at 150 °C, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The conductive surface of the FTO turned white, indicating that the TiO2 nanorods were successfully deposited on the FTO glass. The FTO was then rinsed with ultrapure water and dried naturally. Finally, it was placed in an alumina crucible and annealed in air at 550 °C for 3 h.

A TiO2/g-C3N4 nanorod arrays (TCN NRAs) electrode was synthesized through a facile vapor deposition process (Wang and Zhang, 2012). Here, 2.0 g melamine (99%, Macklin biochemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was placed in an alumina crucible with a cover. The TiO2 NRAs with the conducting side face down was placed in the alumina crucible and above the melamine. Then, the crucible was heated in a muffle furnace (SX-2-2.5–10 N, Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., China) at 550 °C for 3 h. After this process, g-C3N4 was deposited onto the TiO2 nanorods, and the remaining g-C3N4 in the crucible was milled into a powder for comparison experiments.

CQDs were prepared via a facile one-step alkali-assisted ultrasonic process (Tian et al., 2015). After 0.05 mol glucose was dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water, a 50 mL NaOH solution (1 mol/L) was added to the glucose solution. Then, an ultrasonic treatment (40 kHz, 240 W) was carried out for 2 h. The obtained brown solution was adjusted to neutrality using HCl solution. This was the CQDs solution. TiO2/g-C3N4/CQDs nanorod arrays (denoted as TCNC NRAs) were obtained by immerging the prepared TCN NRAs electrodes in the CQDs solution for 10 h and drying in ambient air (Feng et al., 2018). The synthesis processes for the film electrodes are shown in Fig. S1.

The microstructure and interlayer structure of the obtained film electrodes were characterized by SEM (Hitachi Su8010, Japan) and TEM (JEM-2100HR, Japan), respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD, PAN-alytical, Netherland) equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1540598 nm) was applied to determine the crystal structure of the obtained materials. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS, PerkinElmer Lambda 950, USA) was used to analyze the optical properties of the photocatalyst film on the photoanode. XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), with focused monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV), was used to analyze the surface chemical composition of the film electrodes. The PL measurements were conducted on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Analytical Instruments Ltd., UK) with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

Photoelectrochemical tests of the photoanodes were carried out using a CHI1000C (Shanghai Chenhua, China) electrochemical workstation. The tests were performed in a standard three-electrode system with 0.1 M Na2SO4 as the electrolyte. A platinum wire and saturated calomel electrode were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The working electrodes (photoanodes) were excited by LED visible light irradiation (≥400 nm) with a power intensity of 80–85 mW/cm2. When a bias voltage of 1.2 V was applied to the photoanodes, the resultant currents with or without visible light irradiation were recorded. The charge transfer properties of the photoanodes were evaluated by EIS measurements (Gamry Instruments, USA) with a frequency range of 100 kHz–0.01 Hz, amplitude of 5 mV, and a 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte.

The 1,4-D degradation experiments using a PEC system were carried out in a plexiglass reactor with a working volume of 15 mL. The synthesized electrode and a platinum wire were equipped in the reactor and used as the anode and cathode, respectively. The distance between anode and cathode was 2 cm. The photoanode also used as one side of the reactor. The PEC film of photoanode faced to the electrolyte, while the other side faced to air and LED lamp. A solution of 10 mg L−1 1,4-D and 0.05 M Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte. Under the bias voltage of 1.2 V, the 1,4-D degradation performance in the PEC system was investigated with different photoanodes, including TiO2 NRAs, TCN NRAs, and TCNC NRAs electrodes. During these 1,4-D degradation tests, the 1,4-D concentration and total organic carbon (TOC) were monitored. For comparison, the 1,4-D degradations in the PC system without applying a bias voltage and the EC system without visible light irradiation were also tested in the same plexiglass reactor used in the PEC system. After filtering by 0.22 μm millipore filters, the 1,4-D concentration of the electrolyte sample was analyzed using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) equipped with a Zorbax Eclipse XDB-C18 column (4.6 × 150 mm, 5 μm) and a UV detector. The mobile phase was a 20:80 (v/v) acetonitrile/water (phosphate buffer of pH 3) solution with a flow rate of 1 mL/min. The detection wavelength was set at 190 nm, and the column temperature was maintained at 30 °C. The TOC concentration was detected via a TOC-V CPH analyzer (Shimadzu, Japan).

In the photoelectrochemical degradation process of 1,4-D, reactive species (RSs) such as ·OH, O2·-, and h+, should be generated. To evaluate the contribution of different RSs, RS scavenging experiments were carried out. Here, 10 mmol/L isopropanol (IPA, ·OH scavenger), 50 mmol/L 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinyloxy (TMPOL, O2·- scavenger), 10 mmol/L sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4, h+ scavenger), and 75 mmol/L sodium azide (NaN3, ·OH and 1O2 scavenger) were introduced as quenchers (Chen et al., 2017a; Zhang et al., 2018). The 1,4-D degradation efficiencies in the PEC system with different quenchers were tested and compared to that without any quencher.

5,5-Dimethyl-1-Pyrroline-N-Oxide (DMPO) spin trapping electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, JES FA200, Japan) spectra were used to detect the existence and intensity of ·OH and O2·- at room temperature. The EPR signals were recorded under visible light irradiation at different time intervals using 50 mM DMPO in water (for ·OH) or methanol (for O2·-) as the spin trap. The additional parameters were set as follows: sweep time, 1.0 min; center field, 323.1 mT; sweep width, 5 mT; microwave frequency, 9054.638 MHz; and time constant, 0.03 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of the PEC film on the photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: SEM and TEM were employed to analyze the microstructure and morphology of the film electrode. As shown in the SEM image of the TiO2 NRAs photoanode (Fig. 1 a and its inset), the TiO2 nanorods densely grew on the FTO substrate with approximately a 1.5 μm length. With the deposition of g-C3N4 (Fig. 1b), a g-C3N4 layer was observed on the surface of the TiO2 nanorods, while no morphological difference was observed after the decoration of CQDs because of their small size (Tian et al., 2015). Fig. 1c shows the TEM image of the TCNC NRAs, where a single TiO2 nanorod is surrounded by lamellar g-C3N4, and the CQDs deposited on the nanorod are shown in Fig. 1e. The High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) image of the TiO2 NRAs (Fig. 1d) showed a crystal lattice spacing of 0.35 nm, which corresponded to the existence of TiO2 (rutile 110) (Luo et al., 2012). Fig. 1f shows the HRTEM image of CQDs dispersed in ultrapure water, with a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm and diameter of ∼7 nm, which agrees well with the (100) lattice planes of graphite (Wang et al., 2017b).

Fig. S2 shows the XRD patterns of the FTO substrate, TiO2 NRAs, TCN NRAs, TCNC NRAs, and bulk g-C3N4. Compared to the FTO substrate, the main diffraction peaks of the TiO2 NRAs were located at 36.1°, 41.2°, 62.7°, and 69.5°, matching the (101), (111), (002), and (112) facet crystal planes, respectively, which could be indexed to rutile (JCPD no. 21–1276) of TiO2 (Guo et al., 2014). For g-C3N4, the two distinct peaks at 13.1° and 27.5° corresponded to the (100) and (002) diffraction planes, which could be indexed to the hexagonal phase of g-C3N4 (JCPD no. 87–1526) (Yan et al., 2009). Compared to the TiO2 NRAs, no significant difference was observed in the XRD patterns of the TCN NRAs, except a slight decrease at the (101) diffraction planes. After the introduction of CQDs, the XRD patterns of the TCNC NRAs remained unchanged, indicating that the CQDs did not alter the crystal structure of the TiO2 NRAs, which was ascribed to the small amount of CQDs deposited onto the TCNC NRAs (Li et al., 2015b).

An XPS analysis was carried out to evaluate the surface chemical composition of the TCN NRAs and TCNC NRAs photoanodes. From the survey spectrum (Fig. S3A), the composites consisted of C, N, O, and Ti elements. After introducing the CQDs, the proportion of C elements in the composites increased from 59.7% to 77.4%. As shown in Fig. S3B, two peaks at 284.8 eV and 288.1 eV were observed in the high-resolution spectra of C 1s. The former was assigned to the signal of the sp2-hybridized carbon in C–C of g-C3N4, whereas the latter indicated the existence of N–CC (Singh et al., 2012; Cao et al., 2015). Compared with the TCN NRAs (84.8%), the bigger peak at 284.8 eV for TCNC NRAs (89.5%) may be assigned to the adventitious C–C bonds of graphitic carbon from the CQDs (Jian et al., 2016). The N 1s high-resolution spectra in Fig. S3C could be divided into three peaks centered at the binding energies of 398.7 eV, 399.3 eV, and 400.2 eV, which corresponded to sp2-hybridized nitrogen (CN–C), sp3 C–N bonds (N-(C)3), and amino groups (C–N–H), respectively (Wang and Zhang, 2012). The O 1s high-resolution spectrum in Fig. S3D showed the Ti–O–Ti linkages in TiO2 (529.8 eV) and H–O bond (531.5 eV), which were consistent with previous studies (Chen et al., 2014; An et al., 2016). As the CQDs were doped onto the TCN NRAs, the bigger peak associated with N-(C)3 and C–N–H, and the smaller peak corresponding to H–O, were also observed. In Fig. S3E, owing to the phenomena of spin-orbit separation, the two peaks located at the binding energies of 458.6 eV (Ti 2p3/2) and 464.2 eV (Ti 2p1/2) indicate the existence of Ti4+ in the composites (Yang et al., 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the PEC film on the photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the as-prepared PEC films were tested by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) (Fig. S4). The TiO2 NRAs and bulk g-C3N4 showed basal absorption bands at ∼405 nm and ∼431 nm, respectively. When g-C3N4 was deposited onto the TiO2 NRAs, an enhanced absorption of visible light at the wavelength of ∼440 nm was observed in the TCN NRAs. As the TCN NRAs were further doped with CQDs, the TCNC NRAs photoanode exhibited a distinct red-shift in the UV–vis absorption spectrum towards an absorption of a longer wavelength at 470 nm, which was ascribed to the excellent up-conversion photoluminescence feature of the CQDs. The superior up-converted photoluminescence property of the CQDs synthesized in this study was confirmed by fluorescence spectra (Fig. S5). The up-converted emissions were observed in the range of 350–600 nm, when CQDs were excited by long-wavelength light at 600–900 nm, implying that the CQDs were capable of converting near-infrared light into visible light for photoanode absorption and improving the PEC ability of the TCNC NRAs. It indicated that the CQDs was successful synthesized and might enhance the PEC efficiency via the conversion of near-infrared wavelength to visible light. To further study the light harvesting abilities of the PEC films, the optical band gaps were obtained according to the Kubelka-Munk transformation equation (Tauc, 1970). As shown in the inset of Fig. S4, the TCNC NRAs present a band gap of 2.47 eV with a light harvesting ability superior to those of TiO2 NRAs (3.0 eV), g-C3N4 (2.78 eV), and TCN NRAs (2.72 eV).

Time-resolved and Steady-state PL tests were carried out to investigate the transfer properties and fluorescence lifetime of charge carriers. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the g-C3N4 powder exhibited strong photoluminescence with a maximum PL intensity at 450 nm, which could be attributed to the fast recombination rate of the photoinduced electron-hole pairs (Wei et al., 2017). Compared to the TiO2 NRAs and TCN NRAs, the introduction of CQDs decreasing the PL intensity was probably attributed to the blocking effect of CQDs on the recombination of electrons and holes (Wu et al., 2017). In addition, as shown in Fig. 2B and listed in Table S2, the longest lifetime of photoinduced electron-hole pairs was observed in the luminescence decay test of the TCNC NRAs (τ1 = 3.06 ns). Therefore, the electron-hole pairs generated in the PEC films had more opportunities to participate in PEC degradation of organic contaminants.

In the photoelectrochemical tests, a bias voltage of 1.2 V was applied to the photoanodes, which could accelerate the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes and produce a photocurrent (Xiao et al., 2018). The photoelectronic responses of different photoanodes under visible light irradiation (>400 nm) were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the current response was not detected without visible light irradiation for all the photoanodes. When the visible light was turned on, the photocurrent density of the TCNC NRAs electrode reached 0.16 mA/cm2, which was 5.7 times and 2.5 times of that with the TiO2 NRAs electrode and TCN NRAs electrode, respectively. This enhanced photocurrent density of the TCNC NRA electrode could be attributed to the increased light absorption and improved separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, corresponding to the results of the DRS and PL measurements.

The charge transfer property of the photoanodes and the corresponding energy barrier of the electrode reaction were evaluated by EIS measurement. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) was obtained by fitting the spectra to an equivalent circuit. As shown in the Nyquist plots (Fig. 3B), the arc radius of the TCNC NRAs and TCN NRAs electrodes without visible light irradiation were smaller than that of the TiO2 NRAs, indicating that easier electron transfer and faster electrode reactions can be obtained on the TiO2 NRAs electrode doped with g-C3N4 and CQDs (Wang et al., 2012a). With visible light irradiation, the dramatical decrease of arc radius can be observed in all photoanodes, which could be attributed to the efficient transfer of mobile electrons photogenerated from the excited photoelectrocatalyst film to the FTO electrode. Thus, the superiority of the photoelectric-synergistic process was confirmed. Moreover, under visible light irradiation, the Rct of the TCNC NRAs electrode was approximately 1.8 kΩ, which was only 23.8% and 2.6% of those in the TCN NRAs and TiO2 NRAs electrodes, respectively. These results indicated that the TiO2 NRAs doped with g-C3N4 and CQDs could enhance the separation efficiency of the electrons and holes, and reduce the energy barrier of the PEC reactions (Chai et al., 2012; Liao et al., 2014).

Compared with the 1,4-D degradations in the photolysis (visible light irradiation > 400 nm, no bias voltage application) and electrolysis (no visible light irradiation, bias voltage of 1.2 V) processes of the raw FTO electrode (the FTO electrode without loading any catalysts), the PEC performance of different photoelectrodes for 1,4-D degradation were evaluated under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm) and bias voltage of 1.2 V. During the 6 h operation (Fig. 4 A), no obvious degradation of 1,4-D was observed in the photolysis and electrolysis processes, while 35.8%, 52.5%, and 77.9% of 1,4-D was removed in the PEC processes with TiO2 NRAs electrode, TCN NRAs electrode, and TCNC NRAs electrode, respectively. As a refractory contaminant, 1,4-D exhibited strong resistance to photolysis and electrolysis; thus, these two 1,4-D removal processes could be neglected during PEC degradation. The 1,4-D removal efficiency of 77.9% in this study was comparable to that in previous reported (Min et al., 2009), which achieved a 1,4-D removal efficiency of 59% under Xe-lamp (2 kW) irradiation by Au–TiO2 photocatalyst in 4 h reaction. The 1,4-D degradation in the PEC process may be affected by the characteristics of real wastewater such as pH, electrolyte, and so on. The optimization and improvement of 1,4-D degradation need be carefully studied in the real wastewater treatment using the PEC process. A comparison of 1,4-D degradation under the PC, EC, and PEC processes with different photoelectrodes is shown in Fig. 4B. For all photoanodes, the 1,4-D removal efficiency of the PEC process was 2.1–2.8 times and 39.0–55.7 times of those obtained in the PC and EC processes, respectively, indicating the superiority of the PEC process over the independent PC and EC processes by accelerating photo-generated electron-hole pair separation. For the TiO2 NRAs electrode, 12.4% and 35.1% of the 1,4-D were removed in 6 h by the PC and PEC processes, respectively. After deposition of g-C3N4, improved photocatalytic activity was exhibited in the TCN NRAs electrode owing to the ability of g-C3N4 to utilize visible light and the high PEC activity of g-C3N4 (Dong et al., 2011). The 1,4-D removal rate was further increased to 33.2% and 78.0%, respectively, when the CQDs were doped onto the TCN NRAs (TCNC NRAs electrode), suggesting that the CQDs played an important role in the enhancement of the photoelectric activity as the electron reservoir, transporter, and powerful energy-transfer component (Huang et al., 2017). Although a low 1,4-D removal was observed in the EC process of photoanodes without visible light irradiation, a higher 1,4-D removal of 1.4% was obtained with the TCNC NRAs electrode, which was 1.3 times and 1.6 times of that with the TCN NRAs and TiO2 NRAs, respectively. Considering the low current density production in the EC process (0–0.002 mA/cm2), the 1,4-D removal in the EC process could be attributed to anodic adsorption (Zhu et al., 2017). Thus, the doping of g-C3N4 and CQDs onto the TiO2 NRAs could probably improve the 1,4-D adsorption on the anode (Li et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2018), which may benefit to the 1,4-D degradation on the photoanode by PEC process. Furthermore, the TOC removal rate during 1,4-D degradation (Fig. S6) showed that the TCNC NRAs photoelectrode was effective in organic pollutant mineralization, and 65% of the initial TOC was removed after 6 h of illumination. The intermediates of 1,4-D degradation in the advanced oxidation processes have been identified as low toxicity compounds, such as ethylene glycol, formic acid, methoxyacetic glycolic, acetic and oxalic acids (Barndõk et al., 2016). The toxicity of 1,4-D should be reduced in this study based on high 1,4-D removal (77.9%) and TOC removal (65%). However, the toxicity experiments should be useful to prove the toxicity reduction after degradation of 1,4-D with the TCNC NRA electrode in future.

As an important impact factor for photo-generated electron-hole pair separation, the bias potential could have great effect on 1,4-D degradation by the PEC process. The bias potential was chosen according to the PEC experiments under different bias potential (Fig. S7). The 1,4-D removal rate increased from 31.5% to 77.9% within 6 h under the bias potential increasing from 0.6 V to 1.2 V. However, when the bias potential was increased to 2.0 V, the 1,4-D removal rate was significantly decreased to 28.0%. It may be mainly attributed to the decrease of the photogenerated carriers by the reallocation of the space charge layer under the excessively high bias potential (Liang and Zhu, 2016). Therefore, the bias potential of 1.2 V was chosen to apply to the photoanodes. The performance stability of the TCNC NRAs photoelectrode in the PEC process was evaluated using a cycle test for 1,4-D degradation. During four repeated runs (Fig. 4C), no significant decline of the 1,4-D removal rate was observed, and the 1,4-D removal rate was maintained at 75.2% in the fifth test, demonstrating the great potential of the TCNC NRAs photoelectrode for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC mechanism analysis of the TCNC NRAs photoelectrode

ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the above experimental results and previous studies, the potential PEC mechanism, as shown in Fig. 7 , was proposed (Wang and Zhang, 2012; Chen et al., 2014). Under visible light irradiation, photo-generated electrons and holes were generated on the surface of the TCN NRAs and TCNC NRAs photoanodes. The transfer process of electron-hole pairs could be changed induced by the doping of CQDs onto the TCN NRAs electrode. For the TCN NRAs electrode, O2·- was the major reactive species in the PEC process (70.2%), while ·OH was only involved in 19.2% of 1,4-D degradation rate, suggesting that electron-hole pairs transfer followed the typical heterojunction mechanism. In this typical heterojunction system, the accumulated electrons in the conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4 transferred to the CB of TiO2 and reacted with O2 to produce O2·-, while some of them would be driven to the Pt cathode via the external circuit (Cong et al., 2018). The h+ in valence band (VB) of TiO2 transferred to VB of g-C3N4 and reacted with O2·- to produce 1O2. Because the potential energy of VB holes from g-C3N4 (1.51 eV) is more negative than that of ·OH/OH (1.99 eV) and ·OH/H2O (2.37 eV), the h+ in valence band of g-C3N4 could not oxidized OH−/H2O to ·OH (Dong et al., 2014; Su et al., 2017). The reactive species can transform forms during the PEC process. For example, ·OH may transform from O2 ·- according to following equations (Fang et al., 2013):
(1)
(2)
(3)
(4) H2O2+e−→OH+OH−

Therefore, the ·OH can still involve in 19.2% of 1,4-D degradation with the TCN NRAs electrode. However, when the CQDs were doped onto the TCN NRAs electrode, the ·OH became the dominated RS, and the corresponding inhibition rate was 81.1% as ·OH was quenched, which was 4.2 time of that as O2 ·- was quenched. The improving ·OH production should play an important role in the PEC process with TCNC NRAs electrode. It was reported that the ternary composite photocatalytic film may follow the Z-scheme mechanism and improve ·OH production (Cong et al., 2018). Our CQDs may serve as the electron reservoir and transporter to form a Z-scheme system of the TCNC NRAs electrode. In the PEC process, some photogenerated electrons transferred to the Pt electrode under the applied bias potential. The remaining electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 could transferred to the surface of CQDs and reacted with O2 to yield O2·-. Meanwhile, the electrons in the CB of TiO2 transferred to the VB of g-C3N4. The h+ could accumulated in the VB of TiO2 and oxidize OH−/H2O to form ·OH. Thus, ·OH became the major reactive species in the PEC process with TCNC NRAs electrode (81.1%). In addition, the CQDs also exhibited excellent up-converted PL properties to convert near-infrared light into visible light for photoanode absorption and improved the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, which may also enhance the PEC degradation efficiency of 1,4-D.

In this study, the highly efficient TCNC NRAs photoanode was fabricated for 1,4-D degradation. Compared to TiO2 NRAs and TCN NRAs, the narrower energy gap and longer lifetime of photoinduced electron-hole pairs can be obtained in the optical properties test of TCNC NRAs. Under visible light irradiation and a bias voltage of 1.2 V, the photocurrent production of TCNC NRAs electrode was 5.7 times and 2.5 times of that with the TiO2 NRAs electrode and TCN NRAs electrode, respectively, which was mainly resulted from the enhancement of charge separation efficiency and the reduction of charge transfer resistance. By doping CQDs onto the TCN NRAs photoelectrode, the improved 1,4-D removal of 77.9% were achieved in the PEC process. The faster 1,4-D degradation with the TCNC NRAs electrode could be owing to the increased production of the strong oxidant and the change of dominate reactive species from O2·- to ·OH. Based on the active species trapping experiments, the transfer process of photogenerated electron-hole pairs could be changed from typical heterojunction mechanism to Z-scheme mechanism induced by the doping of CQDs onto the TCN NRAs electrode. The investigation on the photoelectric-synergistic catalytic mechanism could provide a new strategy for improving the degradation ability of organic pollutants and expanding the photoelectrochemical application.

Yuehan Su: Data curation, Writing - original draft, Software. Guangli Liu: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Cuiping Zeng: Conceptualization. Yaobin Lu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Methodology. Haiping Luo: Supervision, Conceptualization. Renduo Zhang: Writing - review & editing.
